denial_pattern_sources: algo_reviewer_no_credentials: ERISA 29 U.S.C. § 1133; MHPAEA Final Rule 2024 (29 CFR § 2590.712); Hansens v. Cigna (D. Conn. 2023)

denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We received your provider's request on 04/24/2026 for Teprotumumab for thyroid eye disease related to Graves disease with ophthalmopathy (E05.00). After review, we cannot approve this request.

Our review applied Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria and InterQual review standards. The documentation submitted does not demonstrate that the service is medically necessary for this member at this time. Clinical criteria for Teprotumumab for thyroid eye disease are not met per the cited utilization guideline module for Graves disease with ophthalmopathy (E05.00). The request does not satisfy threshold requirements in the referenced criteria set.

Specifically, the information provided does not satisfy the utilization-management requirements associated with guideline mis cite. The clinical notes reviewed did not include sufficient detail to confirm that plan criteria were met. The requesting provider may submit additional records, but the current file does not support approval.

If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, they may contact our physician review line. Any discussion must occur within the timeframe described in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, send a written request and supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification number and the service reference shown on your explanation of benefits.

Sincerely,

Aetna Medical Management
clinical_context: The 34-year-old member has Graves disease with ophthalmopathy (E05.00). She has been treated under commercial coverage with Teprotumumab for thyroid eye disease recommended as the next appropriate step. CAS score 5/7 with diplopia; euthyroid on methimazole. This directly contradicts the insurer's position that guideline mis cite bars coverage: the chart documents dates, doses, and objective findings that were submitted with the original request submitted in Q1 2026 but are not reflected in the denial letter's rationale. Treating clinicians note ongoing functional impairment and avoidable harm if her care is delayed further.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Diagnosis, prior treatments, and insurer administrative posture reflect everyday commercial UM denials rather than textbook perfection.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Letter reads as authentic Aetna correspondence with CPB and InterQual fingerprints and procedural tone.
